conversation.text: I just finished the quiz and read through my assessment. I clicked to chat with you to learn more.

Hey Jack, glad you're here. Your assessment covered a lot of ground, so let me pull on one thread that I think is especially worth exploring.

That feeling of food sitting in your stomach for hours is really telling. It suggests your stomach isn't breaking food down and moving it along efficiently, which sets up a cascade: poorly digested food feeds bacterial overgrowth further down, which produces the gas and alternating constipation/diarrhea you're dealing with, and those bacteria generate the very compounds most linked to brain fog.

Here's what makes your situation particularly interesting: your eating pattern (essentially fasting most of the day, then a large late meal) may be directly suppressing the stomach acid and enzyme output you need. Gastric acid secretion is partly driven by regular meal signals and adequate thyroid hormone activity, and chronic stress from running on cortisol all day actively inhibits it.

So you may be in a loop where skipping meals worsens the very digestive capacity you need when you finally do eat, which worsens the gut dysfunction, which worsens the brain fog and fatigue that make it hard to prioritize eating well.

One thing your quiz didn't cover that would help me understand this better: when you do eat that large dinner, how soon after do your symptoms tend to flare? Is it immediate, or more like one to three hours later?

That's significant. Symptoms hitting soon after eating point specifically to the stomach itself, not the small intestine further downstream. It suggests your stomach acid and/or gastric motility are genuinely impaired, so food isn't being broken down and moved along properly right from the start.
This fits together well. Research shows that [low stomach acid directly contributes to bacterial overgrowth in the small intestine](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9062108/) by failing to kill off bacteria before they reach the gut. So the stomach isn't just where your symptoms start, it may be where the whole chain begins: poor acid output leads to downstream bacterial overgrowth, which produces the gas, brain fog, and hormonal disruption your assessment outlined.

And here's the connection back to your daily pattern: running on stress hormones all day (from skipping meals) actually diverts blood flow and energy away from digestive function. Then you hit your stomach with the biggest meal of the day at the exact time it's least prepared to handle it.

Do you notice a coated tongue when you wake up in the morning, particularly toward the back?
for the question on Body fat distribution, what did you see as my answer?
Your answer was "Face and neck puffiness." You selected "other" and typed that in rather than choosing one of the standard options.
what other questions did i select other on and type in an answer?
Just one more: for digestive issues, you selected "other" and typed in "Feeling of food sitting in stomach for hours" alongside the standard options you checked.
